title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita, 2022

Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot positions countries by their economic output per person on a logarithmic horizontal axis against their energy intensity on a vertical axis, revealing a distinct negative relationship where higher income generally coincides with lower energy use per dollar of GDP. The vertical scale measures energy intensity in kilowatt-hours per dollar, ranging from 0 to over 4 kWh, while the horizontal axis spans from $1,000 to $100,000 in international dollars at 2011 prices. At the extreme upper end of energy intensity, Turkmenistan, North Korea, and Bahrain cluster above the 4 kWh mark, indicating very high energy consumption relative to economic output. Venezuela and Zimbabwe also appear high on the vertical axis, exceeding 2 kWh per dollar. Conversely, the right side of the chart, representing high-income economies, shows a concentration of points in the lower bands. Norway, Qatar, and Denmark sit near the $100,000 mark with energy intensity levels between 1 and 1.5 kWh. Australia and Canada occupy the middle-right section, with Canada positioned slightly higher in energy intensity than Australia. In the lower-left quadrant, nations such as Burundi, Liberia, and Chad display both low GDP per capita and low energy intensity, falling below 1 kWh per dollar. The color-coded legend identifies regional groupings, with European nations (blue) predominantly occupying the lower-right quadrant of high GDP and low intensity, while African nations (purple) cluster in the lower-left. The data excludes international aviation and figures are expressed in international dollars at 2011 prices. The overall distribution shows that as GDP per capita increases along the horizontal axis, the vertical position of the data points tends to decline, with the highest intensity values concentrated among countries with lower to middle GDP per capita.
